comments.title: A rough and emotionally-turbulent October
comments.content:
Hi John and all,

I don't normally write longer posts or share too-personal details here, but this is unusual. There's no one in my personal life who would relate with these things, and MM readers might find some of this interesting.

Late in September I received the following in an email from an astrologer:

The focus in October is on communication and emotions. At the beginning of the month you will need considerable sensitivity and patience; these qualities will help you harmonize the two areas, especially under the Aries Full Moon. Mid-month, relationship matters take centre stage, and your longing for self-realization grows stronger. The Libra New Moon invites you to dedicate yourself to beauty and harmony. The most intense phase arrives toward the end of the month, when a powerful water trine may set strong dynamics in motion, bringing consequences that are difficult to predict.

I re-read this yesterday after my wild ride of an October. I don't know about everyone else, but this paragraph makes a fairly accurate summary of my month to date.

Here's the story:

A few weeks ago in MM I mentioned that I had a message come to me about a long-lost acquaintance.

This person was a young woman with whom I had an intense, very brief, romantic fling, right at the end of last century when I was a young man. We met through some happy accidents while on a college break, and for a few days we were inseparable. At the end of our trip, we shared contact info. She even made the first move to contact me after we'd gone home. My 19-year-old self decided that his best move was to snub her. I don't even remember why. Knowing who I was back then, I'm confident it was a trivial and likely petty reason. The last day on our trip was the last time I ever saw or spoke to her, which is now over 26 years ago.

The day after receiving and acting on the message to look her up, I got sick with a fever which put me in bed for two days in a half-delusional state. During that time, I vividly relived the memories of my time with her, with full knowledge of what I was going to do. It was like opening up an emotional abscess that had been left to fester for over 25 years.

Ever since, I've felt an overwhelming rush of guilt and remorse and grief and a persistent sadness.

Not unusual in itself, maybe, but I have spent many hours journaling, meditating, in prayer, casting geomantic charts for guidance, and sitting with the memory and the suffering around it. It has helped, some; there have been real insights and healing out of this experience. I get the sense that this was a much-needed reckoning.

I'm fairly convinced that a higher power wanted me to face up to this unaddressed baggage. (Even the Octagon Society work didn't fully get this out of me.) I've left out some personal details and very odd synchronicities, from the past and in this latest episode, that support this.

This experience has rattled me, and I mean big time. I've only had a few of these identity earthquakes in my lifetime and I recognize this as one of them.

It's left me disoriented about most every life decision I made between now and then, and questioning what I want out of my life from here on. Only in the last few days has the emotional upset even started to settle down. The work helped to process, but not to dim the emotional impact.

I considered contacting her to say "sorry", for whatever that might be worth now, but both mundane and occult signs are warning me off that option. I've made my peace with her, and I don't even know the middle-aged woman who has lived an entire life since we knew each other. If she and I are meant to reconcile that way, I'm leaving that in the hands of whatever agency sent this to me. (I doubt anyway that I can pursue it without making it real awkward for everyone.)

But this isn't really about my jilted fling. She's a symptom of older and deeper wounds in my self-image that have festered all this time. The end the 1990s and the early 00s were a dark and nasty time in my life. I didn't like myself, and I acted in ways consistent with that, which made the self-loathing even worse. I'm latching on to her as one of the few joyful memories from an awful time.

I've been grieving for a romanticized life I "could have had" had I been a different man, and feeling regret and guilt for not walking through a door that closed long ago. Then kicking myself all over again for being the idiot who slammed the door shut.

As you always say John, I became attached to my own suffering. My original bad decision to snub her came from habits of self that I can trace back to events in childhood and adolescence. The stinging irony is that those patterns, which have me kicking myself now, are the same patterns that led him/me not to call her back. That's a tough pill to swallow after all this time, but necessary to accept.

Really I'm just sad and very bummed out about the whole thing. When those memories came back so vividly, the decision to snub her feels like it happened a few hours or days ago and I can fix it. When I realize how long it's been, it's a fresh punch in the gut. There's a nagging voice telling me to go make it right, and the helplessness when I can't is triggering intense frustration. I don't see any option but to sit with the feelings and ride it out. I guess that acceptance, and eventually forgiving myself, is one of the lessons I'm meant to take from this.

The emotional wave of guilt and regret has started to ease somewhat in the last 2-3 days after reaching a peak about 5 days ago. That jives with the astrologer's forecast for an intense end of the month.

This may be coincidence, but in that same time, I've also noticed a "loosening up", a greater intensity and quality of the invoked powers, while doing the morning SOP. Things felt more constricted in the earlier weeks of the month.

I don't even know if there's a question in there. I'm so exhausted by it all that I can hardly focus.

Thanks for letting me vent.

replies.title: Re: A rough and emotionally-turbulent October
replies.content: It sounds to me as though you needed to deal with some karma the easy way. Yes, that kind of emotional eruption -- painful and shattering as it is -- is easier than the alternative, which is going into another life with that karma hanging over you and getting smacked in the face by it, perhaps repeatedly, until the lesson is learned. Accept the emotions you're feeling, do your best not to identify with them, and be ready to move onward and live a less burdened life from here on.

replies.title: Re: A rough and emotionally-turbulent October
replies.content:
Thank you John. I know you can't answer this for me, but is there any way to learn if the karma is localized here in this life and my actions in it, or something that's followed me across lifetimes?

I've always had a feeling that something just wasn't right about my life, even though on paper I had most everything going for me. Those dark years I mentioned felt like I was in a hypnotic state and allowing outside agencies to direct everything for me. This is one of the moments where I wake up and ask myself what the heck happened.

replies.title: Re: A rough and emotionally-turbulent October
replies.content: That sounds very much like karma from a previous life. Very few of us are sufficiently free of karma to be able to create much of it in this present life -- we're usually dealing with a long sequence of lives. A good astrologer who takes past lives into account might be able to tell you for sure.

replies.title: Re: A rough and emotionally-turbulent October
replies.content:
This makes sense of something for me. I recently asked Venus for some help becoming more lovable. Since then, every time I've gone to sleep, I've dreamt about someone I used to be really good friends with. I fell in love with her, but I kept it to myself the whole time, because I was convinced she would freak out and that our friendship would die if I ever told her how I felt about her. Then it came out in a spectacularly messy and ugly way, at a terrible time, and she (justifiably, I'm only now realizing) felt like I had lied to her for years, and for a while wanted nothing to do with me. About a year later she decided she missed me enough to try to talk to me again, but I lashed out at her, badly, and I have not spoken to her or seen her in almost a decade now, and almost certainly never will again.

I'd always told myself that she walked away from me twice, but as I've relived the whole experience in these extremely vivid dreams I've realized that is not at all true: I pushed her away twice. I've found myself mourning for the friendship that I destroyed, and longing for what could have been, because she considered me her best friend for most of that time, and she tried to tell me that she wanted more. A number of our friends joked that we were basically a couple, and as I relived that, it felt like a gut punch to realize they were right the whole time. She even tried to tell me she loved me a couple of times, but I completely misinterpreted things, and managed to make it seem like I was not interested in having a relationship with her. She was not made at me because I told her I loved her: she was mad at me because she had tried to make what we had into a relationship, I turned her down, and then years later told her I'd loved her the whole time in a spectacular idiotic way at what was probably the worst possible moment.

I pushed her away because I was convinced I was utterly unlovable. This particular bad habit has roots in childhood emotional trauma, but as I reflect on why I acted the way I did, I find myself realizing that this is far from the only time I have made this particular mistake. It is the most dramatic, but it is far from the only time I did this.

I guess Venus has decided the best way to help me is to make me face the reality of what happened here, which as painful and unpleasant as it is, is probably easier than the alternatives. I suppose I probably need to clear away this karma before I can address the deeper issues. It hurts a lot to realize I had everything I wanted, but was too self centered and focused on my own misery to see it for what it was....

replies.title: Re: A rough and emotionally-turbulent October
replies.content: Magic can be like that. The upside is that you won't make a mistake like that again. That's more of an upside than you may think; a great many souls go stumbling blindly from life to life, making themselves miserable in the same way over and over again, until finally something agonizing enough breaks through and shocks them out of it. You've taken the wiser and braver course, seeking understanding, and the benefits will be considerable.

replies.title: Re: A rough and emotionally-turbulent October
replies.content:
You have my sympathies.

My background is somewhat similar to what you're describing. I did not have much experience at all with women throughout my teens, and my ugly self-image and a lot of the self-loathing was driven by the assumption that I was "bad with girls".

Then I have this one literally fall into my lap (yes, there's a story, and I'm not telling it), and she turns out to be cute and sweet and everything I would have wanted... and I ghost her. For reasons I can't even remember and would probably earn young-me a smack on the head from current-me.

I wish you the best of luck with your own work.

replies.title: Re: A rough and emotionally-turbulent October
replies.content:
The depth of your overlooked grief sounds like much more than the usual kind of missed opportunity for a promising relationship, which we all have somewhere in our memory trove. I can think of two very different reasons that could result in that kind of piercing regret.

The first is that your soul, higher self, or guardian angel may have recognized that young woman as a being you connect with repeatedly in your incarnations. She may have been your father in one life, a sister in another, a guru in another, your child in yet another, etc. In other words, your larger self would have witnessed as your conscious self rejected the opportunity in this lifetime to reconnect with that fellow traveler with whom you have so much history. If that is the case, then it sounds like your soul came away rather disappointed with your conscious choice. You could try using active imagination to uncover what your soul sensed then and what it recommends now.

The second is that your soul, higher self, or guardian angel may have long known how much your tendency to reject and snub life's invitations has been a primary impediment for you this time round. They would have been waiting for you to process enough background baggage to finally be able to deal with a primary habit that potent. If that is the case, then your grief would be coming from beginning to open up the skeleton's closet of all the various missed opportunities that habit has imposed in your life. Fortunately, active imagination would be a great way to decode your soul's wisdom in this case as well.

Whatever it is that has caused such an upwelling of grief, that grief is likely to be the key to a life and a self heretofore unimaginable. Sometimes self-awakening feels like a joyous coming home, while other times it is simply excruciating! May your time of trial and tempering be short... and full of unexpected magic.

— Christophe

replies.title: Re: A rough and emotionally-turbulent October
replies.content:
Thank you Christophe, I appreciate those comments.

It has been painful. I'm only grateful that I've picked up the capabilities to handle the suffering more productively. There was a time when I'd be polishing off a bottle of cheap whiskey and listening to sad songs.

replies.title: Re: A rough and emotionally-turbulent October
replies.content: Not the OP, but having experienced similar feelings in a former relationship: how would you go about active imagination as you mentioned, "to uncover what your soul sensed then and what it recommends now"?

replies.title: Re: A rough and emotionally-turbulent October
replies.content:
There are all sorts of techniques that active imagination can be accessed through. Automatic writing and the kind of unplanned, free-association art created in art therapy sessions are two good ways for adults to dive in. Children tend to turn all their creative play into an expression of active imagination, since whatever issues they're struggling to integrate will naturally show up in their free play.

As adults we tend to be balancing so many unresolved issues that it's hard to know which one the various creative messages might be coming through for. Thus, you will probably want to take a few minutes to meditate on the particular topic you would like your soul to share its insights about, before you formally begin an active imagination technique. Of course, sometimes messages manage to come through all unbidden, such as with Freudian slips, but those can often be confusingly vague as to which unresolved issue they actually pertain to.

If you keep a dream journal and find that your dreams give you worthwhile insights, you could use your dreams as the gateway for active imagination. Again, simply meditate on what question you would like for your soul to address while your conscious mind sleeps. Oh, and always keep in mind that your soul may well consider some of your queries distracting or inconsequential. If the clues you receive through active imagination don't seem to address the question you asked, consider whether your soul might be trying to get you to pay attention to questions you are most assiduously avoiding asking. Our souls really are much brighter than we can hope to ever fully understand.

— Christophe

Sun: Frankincense

replies.title: Re: Three Questions
replies.content: Yeah, I was about to comment: don't sleep on flipping a coin. Decide which outcome is which and charge it with the same intent you would any other divination, and it often does the job.

replies.title: Re: Three Questions
replies.content: I use a divination deck like Tarot for a quick yes or no answer. To do so, I announce my question out loud and say "upright means yes, reversed means no" before I shuffle the deck. Then I shuffle and cut as usual and turn over the topmost card. If it's upright, the answer is yes; if it's reversed, the answer is no. I can often interpret the card itself as telling me why its answer is yes or no.

comments.content:
First of all, thank you for sharing your knowledge. I truly appreciate it. Thanks to that I've been practicing the SOP for nearly a year and my life is still getting better, albeit more slowly than at first.

I have chronic health issues, for context, and sometimes the amount of internal energy moving in the SOP feels overwhelming. Sometimes I have to steady myself throughout, or even perform it sitting down. It seems to raise my body temperature noticeably. There have been a few times when it made me feel really sick, but always temporarily. I would like to continue learning more magic and initiate into a system using the SOP, if divination agrees. However, I am wondering about the effort required for various rituals and how to evaluate it before jumping in, beyond whether or not any particular one is a good idea for me.

So my first question is just that: is there a way to judge how strenuous a ritual will be ahead of time? Although if you have general advice on whether it is a bad idea for people with physical health limitations to practice ritual magic, I would certainly listen to it.

Secondly, I have taken it into my head to learn Bruno's system of memory more or less exactly as he wrote it. And I didn't think it likely that his system has an active egregor, but, well... I have had an experience so far that makes me wonder if it might. I can't give specifics, unfortunately. At any rate, I have not yet begun learning Latin, so I am turning to you, and I hope it's an acceptable question for Magic Monday.

In what sense is Bruno using the words usually translated as subject and adject? As far as I can tell, they are the roots for the English subject and adjective and literally mean the same grammatical ideas. Would it be reasonable to assume that, apart from how he is using them technically for the creation of images, Bruno intends the connotations of subject and adject as grammatical subjects and adjectives?

replies.title: (no subject)
replies.content:
1) It's impossible to tell how challenging a ritual will be for any given practitioner, since that depends on conditions of the subtle body that are difficult to diagnose in our current state of knowledge. All you can do is experiment.

2) The English word "subject" comes straight from Latin subjectum and they mean exactly the same thing. The English word "adjective" -- well, that's amusing in a way, because for some reason English didn't use the Latin word adjectum, which would have given us "adject," but instead took the adjective form adjectivum, which normally calls for a noun -- verbum adjectivum, "a word that functions as an adject," is a long-winded say of saying "adject." Still, in answer to your question, both these words are primarily grammatical and Bruno chose them for that purpose, since the adjects in a memory image relate to the subject image the way that adjectives in a sentence relate to the subject of that sentence.

comments.title: Daily Banishing Rituals
comments.content:
Has anyone to your knowledge ever compiled a list of the various daily protection rituals various traditions use? I am getting interested in starting this as a daily practice. On a related note, for those interested in working within a Christian framework do you think it is better to adapt the sphere of protection to Christianity, or do you think it would be easier to just use one that already came from the Christian tradition?

replies.title: Re: Daily Banishing Rituals
replies.content:
1) I know of no such list, and one would be very useful.

2) It depends on personal preferences. The SoP has very often been adapted to Christian use, but there are also Christian banishing rituals; which one any one person uses really is up to them.

replies.title: Re: Daily Banishing Rituals
replies.content:
Hi JMG (not the OP)


Q1. What kind of Christian banishing rituals do you know of? I suppose the LBRP could be classified as Christian in nature. I also believe that Martinist traditions have some rituals of this kind, but I don't know the specifics.



Q2. Could you use techniques from the grimoires as banishing rituals? I'm referring to the various bathing and praying practices that are used. Are those different in nature?


***PLEASE NOTE*** I'm NOT asking how to get things from spirits. I'm just curious about Christian banishing methods.


Many thanks.

replies.title: Re: Daily Banishing Rituals
replies.content:
1) There are quite a few. Yes, you can use the LBRP as a Christian banishing ritual, but AE Waite (of all people!) came up with a better riff on it: replace the Cabalistic Cross with the ordinary sign of the Cross, and trace a Calvary Cross in each of the four directions, in place of the pentagram, while vibrating the Hebrew name of Jesus, "Yeheshuah." Everything else is as in the LBRP. Christian occultist friends of mine say it's very powerful. There's also this robustly Christian version of the Sphere of Protection:

https://ecosophia.dreamwidth.org/144777.html?thread=17959561#cmt17959561

2) Not really. One of the things that the current fans of grimoire magic have forgotten is that those books were originally written for use by ordained Christian clergy, and assume that you have the full range of medieval or Renaissance Christian sacramental ceremonies handy; those included exorcistic prayers that served the same role as banishing rituals. If you happen to be a devout Catholic and want to go dig up some of the old Renaissance exorcistic prayers, that's certainly an option, but other than that, I'd recommend other options.

replies.title: Re: Daily Banishing Rituals
replies.content:

Many Thanks!

replies.title: Re: Daily Banishing Rituals
replies.content: It's in the degree papers of his version of the Golden Dawn, the Fellowship of the Rosy Cross. Those were published by a small Canadian press a while back, but I got them by being initiated into the Fellowship. (It's ancestral to the branch of the Golden Dawn tradition I inherited from John Gilbert, so I welcomed the chance to reconnect the lineage.)

replies.title: Re: Daily Banishing Rituals
replies.content:

Thanks.

replies.title: Re: Daily Banishing Rituals

comments.title: Relation between astrological gods (planets) and druidic gods (ecological or cultural origin?)
comments.content:
I’ve been studying druidry from your books, and simultaneously learning Hellenistic astrology.

My general sense is that it would be healthy and good magical hygiene to keep the practices relatively distinct and separate. But, I wonder if cultivating a moderate degree of healthy mutual support and synergism is reasonable.

For example, I took advantage of a recent Saturn election to ask support for the consecration of my druid’s wand (Saturn being associated with initiation and cultivation of magical power). The request appeared to be well received, and the ceremony also went very well. I was encouraged that the circulation of light, using the wand, had a newly acquired sense of potency.

In contemplation of the nature of druidic and astrology deities, I feel that the druidic pantheon must be of a different nature than the planets themselves. If the planets have consciousness and embody the archetypes we ascribe them, they are immensely ancient and powerful, and must have been influencing events on Earth from the beginning. I am imagining Mercury initiating the creation of DNA from primordial soup, Venus and Mars initiating the processes of reproduction and competition, Jupiter stewarding periods of evolutionary expansion and innovation, Saturn to mass extinctions, etc…

Even if the druidic gods emerged from ecological forces and processes (the Hawk, Stag, Salmon and Bear of Hu, Sul, Esus, and Elen, for instance), rather than human culture, and are thus millions of years old, they must still be comparatively smaller and younger than the planets, in their great immensity and age. Conversely then, it occurs to me that the druidic gods are closer to us, more likely to notice and appreciate our offerings and devotions, and also, perhaps, more likely to be directly invested in our efforts to live harmoniously within Earth’s ecology. Is it reasonable to explore this line of thinking?

There’s been some good discussion in this space on the nature of gods in occult practice, and of instances where they may merely be egregores. In cases of true deity, that don't happen to be planets, where does the god's consciousness reside and whence did it originate? Being relatively new to all this, there’s probably some context I’m missing. Any suggestions for good general references to improve my understanding, would also be welcome!

replies.title: Re: Relation between astrological gods (planets) and druidic gods (ecological or cultural origin?)
replies.content: Interesting. I tend to think of the Druid gods and goddesses as deities of the earth -- what the Japanese call kunitsu no kami, the kami of the earth -- while the planetary gods and goddesses are amatsu no kami, the kami of heaven. So we're basically using the same scheme. I pray to both, for what it's worth. As for where gods "reside," why, you and I, being mere human beings, are unlikely ever to be able to get a clue about that!

replies.title: Re: Relation between astrological gods (planets) and druidic gods (ecological or cultural origin?)
replies.content: I've been using the same distinction - gods of earth and heaven. That is encouraging confirmation. And, I suppose it didn't hurt to ask! 😆

replies.title: Re: Relation between astrological gods (planets) and druidic gods (ecological or cultural origin?)
replies.content:
This is a very interesting question, and I look forward to JMG's and others' comments on it. I am assuming that when you refer to Mercury, Venus, Mars, etc, you are talking about planetary beings whom we in the European diaspora refer to by the names of Roman deities, but that these planetary beings are not actually the same as the Roman tribal deities with the same names? People in different times and cultures, in the past and now, of course have different names for the planetary beings and sometimes rather different concepts of their natures. For example, Asian, African, and Indigenous cultures call them differently.

I think that in addressing your question, it's important to be clear about these distinctions, and clear about the planetary beings' natures as distinct from cultural overlays. In your descriptions of the planetary powers' actions, it sounds like you're thinking of them more like the Lords of Flame, Form, and Mind that our host discusses in his writings. One approach to your question that might make sense is regarding both the planetary beings, and the Deities recognized by human cultures, as members of those Lords, working perhaps on different areas or levels of activity. Just a thought.

Winifred Hodge Rose

comments.title: Brain death, vegetative states & Occult theory
comments.content:
Hi John Michael, 

Yesterday a friend of mine was found unconscious and not breathing.  Although he was revived, he is for all intents and purposes brain dead and is being kept alive by machines. There is a slim chance that he may recover enough to be in a permanent vegetative state, though this is unlikely. 

As distressing as it was to see him in this state, there was a part of me that felt "he" (whatever that means) had already left his body, though I wonder if this was my way of avoiding dealing with the reality of death.


What does Occult theory say about death in the context of brain death or remaining alive in a vegetative state? Is the soul or identity considered to remain in the body if brain death has occurred? Can you suggest books to learn more about this? 

I have to say that the whole experience left me shaken up and questioning my beliefs around death and survival after death. Is there an Occult practice that can help one prepare for the moment of their own death? 

Thanks as always, 

P

replies.title: Re: Brain death, vegetative states & Occult theory
replies.content:
1) According to occult teachings, once brain death takes place, the soul is gone. The body can be kept more or less intact in a state of vegetative life, but there's no one home.

2) Begin by reading up on occult ideas of death -- Dion Fortune's book Through the Gates of Death is a good source for this, and can be downloaded free of charge here:

https://www.globalgreyebooks.com/through-the-gates-of-death-ebook.html

Then let yourself think about it. Keep the occult idea of death (the separation of the soul from the material body) in mind, replacing the ignorant materialist idea of death (the annihilation of the person). You might also visit your public library and see what it has in the way of books about near-death experiences. The best way to prepare for death is to understand it, because then you won't fear it, and when it happens, you can then deal with it constructively and go on to the next stage of your existence.

replies.title: Re: Brain death, vegetative states & Occult theory
replies.content:
1) What happens in the rare cases where someone does recover from brain death?

I suppose in some cases, the pronouncement of brain death was simply premature: brain activity dipped below detectable levels but didn't completely cease.

replies.title: Re: Brain death, vegetative states & Occult theory
replies.content: Sometimes the soul returns to the body. Sometimes it never left. Very, very rarely, another soul enters the body to complete a life interrupted in another body.

replies.title: Re: Brain death, vegetative states & Occult theory
replies.content: 1. Does this mean the etheric body is still fully attached to the physical body as long as it's being kept alive artificially? And presumably the astral body separates from both at the moment of brain death?

replies.title: Re: Brain death, vegetative states & Occult theory
replies.content: Yes. If the etheric body leaves, the material body shuts down, no matter what steps are taken to preserve it: the organs stop functioning, the blood congeals, and putrefaction sets in. The astral body serves as the vehicle of the soul, and takes the soul with it when it leaves.

replies.title: Re: Brain death, vegetative states & Occult theory
replies.content: I just read Plato's "Phaedo" which has a lot to do with this. A remark in there by Socrates (he's been sentenced to death and is about to drink the hemlock in this dialogue) is "It is self evident that the soul is immortal. A man should repeat this like an incantation".

replies.title: (no subject)
replies.content:
There's a line of theorizing about anger within evolutionary psychology. The basic claim is that anger is the lever that certain instincts pull in situations where those instincts expect that there's probably some other animal whom it'd be in the interests of one's own blood to convince that one has more at stake during otherwise-non-conflict circumstances than it expected and is therefore more willing to resort to conflict, and/or convince that it would come off worse (or oneself would come off better?) in a conflict (whatever kind of conflict is in the offing) than it expected. Both of these quantities are associated with getting more favorable outcomes in negotiated alternatives to conflict. (This is argued in terms of a standard game-theoretic baseline strategic structure involving efficient outcome frontiers and bargaining equilibria.) (Meanwhile, having "be in an otherwise-costly state that makes you good at conflict" be downstream of the lever also helps with getting more favorable outcomes in the event that the conflict actually happens.)

The part about "more at stake than the other animal expected" would connect with the fear and pain themes. The part about "the other animal stands to come off worse than it expected" might relate to the idea that instincts have to pick between anger/resentment and fawning/submission/anguish, because when one has no leverage nor any prospect of leverage even with allies, one's instincts can simply skip over the option of anger and go with submission or anguish. It might also relate to the idea that instincts might set up one's intuitions about pride/ego/honor/reputation in such a way as to make one feel fear or pain when they think one stands to lose reputation or honor by coming to be thought of as weaker than one is, especially where this would embolden potential opposing coalitions. If that was how the part about "the other animal stands to come off worse than it expected" worked mechanically behind the scenes, by converting detection of such a circumstance into fear and pain using a particular instinct-emplaced structure of ego attachments, then in that case both functions of anger could work through elaborations of the mechanisms for fear or pain.

JMG: Importantly, this would also be a core biological function of "ego" which one would not at all think to expect from just the claim "ego is nothing more than one's beliefs about oneself". For this or other reasons, ego might be specifically highlighted and provided for by various instincts as a thing to weave various schemes around. The various people who you've seen arguing that everyone should just get rid of egos may be wrong not primarily because one can't help but have beliefs about oneself, but because there are a ton of instincts that are going to keep flailing about trying to point at some rudimentary substitute for a coherent ego, and those instincts are probably going to succeed at doing that because they're pretty strong.

(A notable alternate configuration of the relation between anger and fear, to try inhabiting to investigate their relation, is blustering. In blustering, one is trying to look prepared and willing to engage in conflict, but also to escape or submit if one's bluff is called, but also to actually engage in the conflict if that doesn't work.)

replies.title: (no subject)
replies.content: No, it's not a core biological function of ego. We have no reason to think that the less complex animals have ego, for example, but they're certainly capable of anger. The anger response is one of the elements of which ego is built, once you have a consciousness complex enough to have a conscious self-image.

replies.title: (no subject)
replies.content:
The matter is not one of the existence of a capacity for anger, but the existence of a link from "this other animal doesn't think it stands to lose as much as it should" to fear/pain. To the extent that there were forms of anger that didn't route through fear or pain, then there would not be a necessity for a mechanism to coordinate that routing, and therefore no reason for ego to be a candidate component of that mechanism.

I'm trying to think through the edge case of bees. I suppose bees might have the ability to juggle decisions about how intimidated e.g. a bear seems, but it might work through the simple stigmergy of constantly judging how close the bear is, and accumulating that judgement partly in the form of alarm pheromone signals to each other... but maybe the alarm pheromones just press the buttons for pain and fear... These concepts are kind of slippery.

(Supposedly it's dangerous to have ripe bananas around bees because they interpret a component of banana scent isoamyl acetate as a bee attack signal.)

replies.title: (no subject)
replies.content: In a great many cases, well up into the midrange vertebrates, a strong case can be made that it's just a matter of innate triggers setting up evolutionarily selected reactions. Again, the ego is a much more complex thing? That said, of course, "ego" is simply a word, and its meaning is therefore defined by usage, not by anything more significant. If you want to define it in a different way from the one I use, by all means.

comments.title: Día de Muertos
comments.content:
A couple Magic Monday's back I publicly grumbled at no one in particular about the recently minted bad back-translation of Día de Muertos, the Mexican festivity held on November 2nd every year honoring the dead, the death process and welcoming your family ancestors back home. The mistake comes from translating the original to the English as Day of the Dead, back to Spanish so it sounds "authentic". In that process the article 'los' got added giving the cacophonic Día de los Muertos, but don't go about saying that in México if you don't want a load of stereotypes dumped on you. Yes, I am aware that is how it's been spelled in the U.S and elsewhere, it's wrong.

There is more than mere puritanism and vanity to my annoyance with the term, besides the butchering of my culture for the sake of the market, which is bad enough. If you add the article 'los' you are saying the day is of the dead, not about death, which gives it a distinctly creepy vibe, probably because of the usual morbid reaction to death, Droolywood movies, and the fascination with anything that would spook someone's mother. That is rather infantile and goes against the very purpose of the festivity, which is not about entertaining foreigners or putting someone or something from a Mexican background on a place that generates less guilt, but about Light, the afterlife and feeling comfortable and lighthearted with what is usually a grim topic. The vibe of it is much closer to the vibe of the Hindu festivity Diwali, another festival of Light held around the same dates, with a similar purpose --bringing out the light in a time when darkness can press around the edges, but eventually is pushed back. You can find another example in Día de Reyes, the festivity commemorating the visitation of the three magi to Jesus child, its not Día de los Reyes, because the day doesn't belong to them, its about them. For example, its Independence Day, not Day of the Independence or the Day of Christmas.

You see in México we laugh at death, we even worship Death (think of Santa Muerte) and that is culturally different enough that it generates confusion and raises some hairs. Sometimes its bad, like the person who would insist to my face that it's a demonic thing and then tried cursing me, which ended up bad for her, justifying her dumb beliefs. That is a normal process I guess, specially when something hits the mainstream but I think it is important in such a time to bring about the purpose and the ways of the tradition, a living tradition, not an anthropological curiosity, so that people can connect with it.

If you have beared with my dense yapping so far, I sincerely thank you.

With that preamble, I would like to share with you all, and I mean it sincerely, how to do a simple celebration if that is of interest to you. We Mexicans didn't use have the concept of cultural appropriation which I think is rather dumb so we don't mind about it, but we do care about things done the right way because otherwise it feels like things are being made fun of as if it was a game or something, which is rather rude.

An offering of Día de Muertos has some elements. The first are flowers. Traditionally cempacúchil (Sem-pah-SUE-chill), a sibling of the marigold is used to decorate the whole thing. Cempa means 20 and xochitl means flower, so it is the many petaled flower. A fragant and seasonal flower should be fine. If you can't find orange flowers, such as St. Johns wort, add some other orange thing like a pumpkin.

On this date, we see death not as an absence of life but a living presence and we invite both cultural heroes and familiars to the altar and talk to them, get drunk with them or share a meal. So you need pictures of the person and some element that represents him or her, like an item or a famous saying. You also need food and drink. So you can prepare their favorite meal and offer it to them, as well as pouring them a glass of their favorite spirit or a few cigarettes. Sugar or chocolate skulls are also very traditional. Leave this food on the altar from Nov. 1st to Nov 3rd. You can cover it with plastic wrap to prevent bugs but open it when you are sitting at it. You can bring a plate or drink for you. Do NOT eat the food, drink or cigarettes on the altar afterwards, as crazy as it may sound, it has been eaten already by your ancestors or someone representing him so its energetically depleted and will make you sick. Bury it, throw it away respectfully or compost it.

The next thing are called Calaveritas Literarias (You could translate them as literary skellies) and they are short or long poems about death, the dying or someone, living or dead, written in a mocking but joyful manner. For example if you put the picture of Elvis, make it about Elvis. Here are some not particularly good examples I sent to my cousin celebrating it with other people in Denmark:

Huesuda Fries
The ungrateful get the scythe
And the weary stumble in fright
La huesuda takes only
The ones who don't order
Fries on the side!
(Huesuda means bony one, that is to say, death.)

Catrina
La Catrina comes and goes
Speaking into the ears of woes
She likes to run errands fast
But alas, she just came and passed

She saw you and said:
Nah, she's still waiting for the best

Crook Soldier
On the side of the river:

The ghost of a woman waited
For her husband lay dead in the war
She rather stay here and wait it
Than seeing that crook and sore!

The other element are candles, lots of candles, preferably veladoras, which are candles on a glass casing. These are arranged both on the altar and in front of your house on the sides of the entrance, either one on either side or several, like a path. This is done so that your dead know the way to your house and further down to your altar.

Another element are, again, sugar or chocolate skulls but with your name on them, or someone's else's. You gift these as a reminder that you too shall die.

Incense is another important element, specially if you are going to be doing the actual sitting and talking to your dead ones. Copal is traditional, frankincense works. If you do, sit down at night next to your altar, light the candles and incense, uncover the food and talk. Talk just as if you were talking to them, either to the heroes or your family members. Pour them a drink, have some yourself and just hang out, and do some listening and quiet sitting as well. When you are done, thank them. You can do this any day from about mid october to Nov 2nd which is the culmination. Then send your ancestors or heroes on their way back with a blessing and take the altar down.

I think that is all.

Here is an example of an offering done to David Lynch and others at the National Film Library this year.

[IMAGE:0]

replies.title: Re: Día de Muertos
replies.content: I'll be attending a Day of the Dead celebration this year, as I did last year; a good friend of mine is married to a Mexican woman, and she always hosts a party for that day. The interesting thing is that she calls it Dia de los Muertos. Spanish has no shortage of dialects, and I wonder if this is a dialect difference.

replies.title: Re: Día de Muertos
replies.content: That may be it, there are even some ancient dialects of Spanish still spoken. I've never heard it before until last year though and I have associated it with other things. Since it's been two or three people now that say it so, I may be wrong. Perhaps in the center of mexico is this way? I certainly don't like it, but I have been a little irritable lately, for which I apologize, so that could be why.

replies.title: Re: Día de Muertos
replies.content: My friend's wife's family comes from Sinaloa, though they live in and around Guadalajara these days. I don't know how this fits with the dialect theory -- I'm lamentably ignorant about Mexican geography -- but thought I would mention it.

replies.title: Re: Día de Muertos
replies.content:
It's very much appreciated, specially your openness and kindness. I was in one of the historic neighborhoods of Mexico City yesterday and had to deal with the fact that all of it is foreigner owned now, effectively being outpriced from one of my lifelong dreams. I overheard several real estate agents talking about frivolous things while walking down the streets, pointing at things as if on a safari or a mall. That made me angry as well as seeing what I consider something beautiful being commercialized. But it's not mine, it's of the people and that includes the people we welcome here and its variations, to my liking or not.

Regardless of spelling, I wish to share it openly. The important fact is understanding, visibility and being sincere, for as much as it got tinged with some anger. That is not way. I just feel that in leaving home for some other place, I ended up nowhere familiar, but grasping to a dead corpse doesn't help. Seeing it under a new light does.

Much to work on. Specially if I am thinking on liking the woman that stole my heart like a bandit, who went back to Seattle yesterday after I gave her a painting I did for her birthday. Sigh. You know, one of the downsides of psychism is that for the ones who haven't mastered the art yet, things tend to get mixed up in confusion sometimes. The old is dead, and the new still awaits to be reborn.

comments.title: Daylight savings & natal charts
comments.content:
apologies in advance if this has been covered before.

How does one take into account daylight savings time when calculating astrology?

Thanks for your help and your time.

replies.title: Re: Daylight savings & natal charts
replies.content: Most computer programs automatically correct for it, subtracting the hour as needed, in the process of correcting for the other ways clock time differs from solar time. If you're doing it the old-fashioned way, by hand, there are tables where you can look up the dates of daylight savings time in different locations and correct for it.

replies.title: Re: Daylight savings & natal charts
replies.content:
Maybe I'm missing something obvious but is daylight savings even something that has to corrected for specially? If you want to take sidereal time from a table, you have to convert to GMT anyways.

—David P.

replies.title: Re: Daylight savings & natal charts
replies.content: If you get somebody's birth time, it's almost always given in local clock time, which includes daylight savings in the relevant months. Yes, you have to convert that to GMT, but correcting for daylight savings is part of that.

replies.title: Re: Daylight savings & natal charts
replies.content: Things like this is why any computer program that stores dates is strongly advised to store all times as UTC aka GMT. You can always convert back to local time but trying to guess the time zone of a local time from context is a recipe for problems.

comments.content:
I'd like some assistance crafting an affirmation. I don't normally practice magic, and this is my first time doing this sort of thing.

I am looking to get back into art, specifically drawing and painting, after many years of not practicing. You've said in the past that affirmations work best when they focus on the ultimate goal, but I haven't decided whether I want to pursue it professionally or anything else particularly lofty because I don't think I will have a clear enough idea of what I even want from my art until I've been drawing again for a while. So for now, my goal is to simply build a habit of drawing every day, in my sketchbook. I would like this to become an ingrained habit, akin to meditation or brushing one's teeth.

Over the past few days, I've already been keeping up the habit of drawing daily through simple willpower, but I'd like to see if an affirmation might help the process along.

What follows is just to give context that may be helpful in creating the affirmation. If it's too much context, I apologize:

Per some advice I've seen here in the past, I did some journaling to determine what my blockage was when it came to practicing art, why I've stopped for so many years. In the end, it came down to an unwillingness to take risks, due to a fear of failure (a common enough reason, as far as I know). I became trapped in a cycle that more or less went like this:

try to start drawing again >> fear of failure sets in >> don't take risks >> don't improve or grow >> feel stagnant, like I'll never improve >> get discouraged >> quit >> repeat

Common ways for me to avoid taking risk were drawing subjects I already knew how to draw, in poses I have drawn before, doing simple exercises to "practice fundamentals" instead of drawing creatively, and doing studies instead of pursuing my own original ideas.

Anyway, to put it more simply, my goals are as such, in order of priority:

1. Draw in my sketchbook everyday
2. Ideally, find a degree of joy in the process of doing so
3. I would like to improve over time (trying new things, not stagnating)
4. Over time, I would like to incorporate additional materials (ink, watercolors, etc) as I get comfortable

It is my impression that affirmations need to strike a balance between being specific and being simple, so I'm unsure of how much detail to include. Would something like "I enjoy drawing in my sketchbook every day" be enough? Or maybe something like "I draw and paint beautifully in my sketchbook every day"?

"I enjoy drawing and painting in my sketchbook every day, consistently trying new things and improving" feels more comprehensive but perhaps it's too unwieldy? Maybe it's fine, I simply don't know enough to say.

Affirmations aside, any other advice you or others could provide that would be helpful in building this habit would also be appreciated. Thank you in advance.

Once you've done that for a year, and have the habit of drawing well established, you can change affirmations if needed.

replies.title: Re: Horoscope Repair

comments.title: Various questions
comments.content:
1) Is Hermeticism the original self-help?

2) Is there more JMG in Ariel or Bernard Moravec?

3) And, I live in Vienna’s Mariahilf district. Should I be afraid of werewolves?

replies.title: Re: Various questions
replies.content:
1) No, it was a reformulation of much older traditions.

2) That's a complex question. I don't model my characters on myself, and their personalities always have quirks I didn't expect when I started writing -- for example, Ariel gets angry in situations where I get depressed, and her habit of self-mockery isn't mine; Bernard's calm, on the other hand, is something I'd like to have, but generally don't! Ariel's difficulties with learning magic, though, are very closely modeled on those I faced forty years ago.

3) No, they got what they came for and left with it. As far as I know, the werewolves of Vienna mostly live in the Döbling and Floridsdorf districts, and spend the night of the full moon each month in the Wienerwald.

replies.title: Re: Various questions
replies.content:
1) Interesting! But, is the formulation of "self-help" akin to the truth? (I know those traditions are much more than that, of course).

2) Interesting as well... I also meant by the question if the facts of those characters' paths resemble your own career?

3) Errrr... I'm not really relieved. Are you freaking serious? Friends and I like to spend time in Wienerwald, and have our seasonal rituals at the beginning of it.

Interesting that Döbling is a very affluent district and Floridsdorf a very working class one. Are there Werewolves in all strata of society?

(And, how on earth did you get to know about the Austrian werewolf scene?)

replies.title: Re: Various questions
replies.content:
1) Well, you have to do it yourself -- Hermeticism isn't one of those traditions that rely on someone else to save you. So it's self-help in that sense.

2) Not really. I didn't have an occultist grandfather to take me under his wing and guide my steps on the path, so I fumbled and stumbled a lot; nor, unfortunately, do I have children, much less grandchildren.

3) Er, we're talking about a novel, you know. I learned about the Austrian werewolf scene the same way I learned about the internal politics of the Heydonian Institution and the next volume of the Bertie Scrubb novels: that is, I made it up. Novelists do that.

If there are werewolves in the Wienerwald, by the way, you have nothing to worry about in the daytime and nothing more to worry about 29 nights out of each month. On the night of the full moon, they'd be active, but that doesn't mean they'll be thirsting for your blood. If the old wolf-shamanism has survived, they'll have had to become very elusive, and corpses with their throats ripped out are too likely to alert the authorities.

replies.title: Re: Various questions
replies.content: I see :-) Have you ever visited Vienna? If not, would love to have you here!

replies.title: Re: Various questions
replies.content: I was there briefly in 1977, in one of those too-fast tours of Europe. I'd like to visit it again -- Vienna's one of the three cities in central Europe I'd most like to see. (The others are Prague and Cracow -- all three have stellar places in the history of Western occultism.)

comments.title: Question on the SOP and the definite article

Concerning invocations in the SOP, I noticed in the recent past and have been wondering on when calling the elements the definite article is used, e.g. "I call on the Earth and all its powers" but not for the spirits. "I call on Spirit Below and all its powers".

replies.title: (no subject)

I wish my late wife was still around to answer this -- she had a prolonged and difficult menopause and ended up working out various ways to deal with the considerable emotional and spiritual effects of the process. One thing that helped her a great deal was creative work -- she'd always been a musician, but once menopause arrived she started playing the fiddle daily, and kept it up until shortly before her death.

Dear JMG,


You mentioned in a recent MM that Dion Fortune didn't use linear initiation through the sephiroth (or something to that effect). I think you said that the GD does use such a progression and that it can cause problems.


Q1. What problems can the GD approach to initiation cause?

replies.title: (no subject)
replies.content:
1) The problem with the GD system of initiation comes with the Inner Order, and specifically with the Adeptus Minor degree. Very few people in any generation are ready to make the transition to the mode of consciousness symbolized by Tiphareth. If you aren't ready for it, and take the ritual anyway, all that solar force goes straight to the ego, with predictable results. That's what happened to Aleister Crowley; everyone in the London GD realized that he wasn't even remotely prepared for the Adeptus Minor initiation, but Mathers gave it to him anyway, after which Crowley promptly changed from an ordinarily annoying young man to the shrill and overbearing dweeb we all know. More generally, that's why Golden Dawn temples so reliably blow sky high as a result of ego conflicts.

2) She took the system of three degrees she'd received from her teacher Theodore Moriarty, which was based on an offshoot of Freemasonry, and modified them to relate to the three currents she worked -- Cabalistic, Egyptian, and Greek Pythagorean -- without any reference to the Sephiroth.

3) She drops some hints in a few places in her essays. I know what I do because I've received some unpublished documents, which I don't have permission to publish. Sorry.

Luke, if this is the African Grey Parrot Alex who has been the subject of Irene M. Pepperberg's research for a good number of decades now, then the story is not true -- alas!

My wife and I have been good friends with Irene for decades now, and my wife has been closely following Irene's scientific work with Alex ever since its early days. So Irene called us about Alex's death within a day or so of when it happened.

Alex died overnight (of heart failure) in the lab where he lived, with no people around him at the time. The lab manager found him dead in his cage the next morning. As you can imagine, it was a horrible shock for Irene, who was very close to Alex, and spent hours with him almost every day of his life.

This is not to say that Alex might not have asked such questions as he died. He was very talkative with people whenever he wanted to know something (and not only when he wanted to get something from people near him). But if he did ask, there was no one around to hear and answer him.

My wife will ask Irene tomorrow whether Joe Rogan had ever met Alex, or had interviewed her about Alex. Magic Monday will be over by then, but I'll post a follow-up next Monday.

As for African Greys and self-harming when they lack intellectual stimulation or sympathetic social interaction, that's certainly true. They are extremely intelligent and sociable birds, and if there aren't other parrots around, they will socialize with people instead. An African Grey lived with us in our house for a few decades, and we got to know her quite well. When she was dying, we were there with her, and it was clear that she was fighting hard against what she was experiencing as she died. But she didn't have Alex's language skills, and didn't ask us any questions in English. She died in the morning of Christmas Eve, and that holiday season has always been hard for us to get through ever since.

replies.content: Angel magic isn't suited to beginners. It's an intermediate-to-advanced branch of magic and requires a great deal of preparatory training, especially in meditation and the elements of ritual. It also requires considerable personal spiritual development. You can't communicate with angels unless you can rise to their level at least briefly. (In the same sense, communicating to demons is the easiest thing in the world -- all you have to do is sink to their level, and there they are. Mind you, getting back is much harder...)

Attaining the level of consciousness at which your personality survives physical death is also advanced stuff. So is the kind of large-scale thaumaturgy that shapes the destiny of nations; the work the occult wing of the Nazi party did to redefine the future of Europe was extremely advanced, more so than they could handle (fortunately!), and Dion Fortune's masterly countermagic against them was also the work of a very advanced occultist. replies.title: Re: Insomnia treatment other than ritual
replies.content: I always have insomnia when there are emotional blocks close to the surface / pressing for release. I find that if I drop down into my body and allow the block to release the insomnia usually resolves once the trauma is cleared from my system. Your situation may be different, but I would highly recommend some type of somatic release therapy or practice.

replies.title: Re: Insomnia treatment other than ritual
replies.content:
Do you have a regular mundane bedtime routine? Turn off the screens a couple hours before sleep, do something fairly boring (watch the fire, not read the latest novel!), have a cup of warm milk with a drop of vanilla, brush your teeth? Something like that.
Follow with appropriate bedtime prayers to Whomever you pray to, perhaps a memory verse, then off to bed.
It can be hard to train a busy brain to shut down! I used to have this problem in college, decades ago, and the above plus playing instrumental music softly all night worked. (This last is often contraindicated by a spouse's need for sleep.)
Now, with children all in double digits, I find my biggest hindrence to a good night's sleep is the standard introvert's need to have alone time to recharge. Enforcing bedtime is a solid key there.
Hope you find some rest!
BoysMom

replies.title: Re: Insomnia treatment other than ritual
replies.content:
I'm not sure about insomnia, but I did a lot of long distance travel for work, and suffered from some pretty severe jet lag when I was younger. The problem was that sometimes I needed to sleep during what my body thought was the middle of the day, so it would not cooperate until I found a way to tell it "This is time to sleep!"

I got a sleep mask, and then got into the habit of wearing it to bed when I needed to sleep. I'd set an alarm for whatever time I needed to get up, and then, no matter what, did not get up until then. This worked wonders for me, and solved my jet lag issues. Since jet lag is similar to insomnia, I think it might be worth trying as well.

replies.title: Re: Insomnia treatment other than ritual
replies.content:
not magical, but there are lots of things to try. A pad of paper and pencil by your bed so you can jot down things that keep going thru your mind so you dont have to keep thinking about them while you try and go to sleep; do not have any screen time with tv, computer, phone for an hour or two before you try and go to sleep; Warm milk, no cocoa in it; if you read before bed, switch to something you already have read or just something that itsnt too exciting; put an open small jar with vinegar in it on the nightstand; mist your pillowcase with lavander water; do a cool water sponge bath all over before bed; there are various things to ingest to try to calm the mind, just do one of them, I like the traditional chinese medicine remedy spirit calm on the occasions this comes up, https://www.activeherb.com/anshen/ I used to take the vitamin supplement tryptophan, or an Albizia tincture.

I have been falling asleep well lately, but do some things on that list and have spirit calm in the house for if it is needed.

The entire process is also based on exchange -- as the Romans said, do ut des, "I give that you may give."

As for people trying to wheedle or bully spirits into giving them goodies, I'm not under any illusion that I'm going to stop them from running down that path. I'm just not willing to help them, and that's for a number of reasons. Among them is that the people who posted here, trying to get me to help them get goodies from spirits, were doing so in a very dysfunctional way. They'd post questions about how to get this from that spirit, and then they'd post about how their attempts to get that from this other spirit hadn't worked, and then it was on to the next spirit; when I said, "Maybe you should try some other kind of magic," they didn't hear it at all. It was all spirits, all the time, and the mere fact that they were failing over and over again didn't get through the yard-thick concrete inside their skulls. I saw (and see) no reason to facilitate failure.

comments.title: CGD Ovate Vows and DA Philosophy
comments.content:
1. I was looking at the Ovate oath in the CGD, and I noticed it prohibits "selfish" use of the magical techniques it teaches. It's possible this question is entirely due to my personal baggage around the word "selfish," but i was wondering, what kind of workings would this oath prevent? I feel like self development isn't necessarily selfish, but there may be a very fine line there.

2. Is it okay to add in the central ray while still in the ovate grade if we have practiced the LRP for a while? I was feeling the need to do that.

3. In the Dolmen Arch philosophy, there seems to be a tension between an eternal recurrance similar to Nietzsche's ideas and the possibility of transcendence. On one hand, there is the idea of Awen as the only eternal reality in an endless series of cosmic days and nights, and on the other there is a notion of being beyond time in Cuegant. Any tips on squaring these ideas? Or is this more of a meditation theme?

Thanks!

replies.title: Re: CGD Ovate Vows and DA Philosophy
replies.content:
1) "Selfish" has a very specific definition. You can help yourself and not be selfish -- most people who work for a living do this. Selfishness consists of benefiting yourself at the expense of others. Doing that with magic is that the oath prohibits.

2) Yes, you can do that.

3) It's a meditation theme. The resolution of that tension requires a higher state of consciousness, and meditating on it can help you get there.

comments.title: Ancestor worship
comments.content: Several months ago, while hiking, I discovered a Native American site with evidence of 6,000 years of continuous usage (based on arrowheads, pottery, etc.-Helene caused a lot of topsoil erosion.)  I haven’t disclosed its location to anyone and I doubt I will simply because I feel like it needs to be left undisturbed.  The Native Americans who lived here (The Yadkin) no longer exist in any real sense.  Prior to the advent of roads and the introduction of the horse it would have been exceptionally difficult to reach the location so I imagine it had some sort of spiritual significance.  I enjoy the spot as a place for meditation/contemplation. 6,000 years of human occupation reduced to forgotten and overgrown detritus scattered about puts things in perspective.  What is the best way to honor the ancestors of a place when they are not your blood? Thank you-Croatoan

replies.title: Re: Ancestor worship
replies.content: I honestly have no idea. The Yadkin will have had their own traditions, but those will have vanished when they did.
